treatments.name: Wide Surgical Excision with Negative Margins

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple large retrospective series and NCCN/ESMO guidelines establish surgery as first-line curative intent. No RCTs possible given rarity, but consistent Level 2A evidence from institutional series of 100-160+ patients showing surgery is the only curative modality

survival_benefit.rationale: Complete resection with negative margins is the strongest predictor of survival. 5-year OS 20-35% in resectable disease vs <10% without surgery. T3N0M0 with 4mm thickness has moderate risk; clear margins improve local control by 40-50% vs positive margins

accessibility.rationale: Available at all tertiary head and neck surgical centers. Requires experienced head and neck or oromaxillofacial surgeon. Oral tongue location permits adequate access though reconstruction may be needed for large defects

safety_profile.rationale: Operative morbidity includes speech/swallowing impairment depending on excision extent, wound healing complications, and flap failure risk if reconstruction needed. Generally well-tolerated in 45-year-old with good performance status

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — surgery does not depend on molecular profile. Appropriate for all mucosal melanoma patients with resectable disease regardless of BRAF/NRAS/KIT status

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision removes the primary tumor with wide margins (target >=1cm where anatomically feasible) to eliminate all malignant cells. In oral tongue location, may require partial glossectomy with or without free flap reconstruction depending on defect size.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-term treatment outcomes of mucosal melanoma of the head and neck: 161 cases (Jethanamest et al.)

notable_side_effects: speech impairment (variable, depends on extent of glossectomy)
treatments.availability: Standard of Care — available at all head and neck surgery centers worldwide

treatments.name: Adjuvant Nivolumab (Anti-PD-1 Monotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate-238 demonstrated adjuvant nivolumab superiority over ipilimumab in resected stage III/IV melanoma (HR 0.71). Mucosal melanoma patients were included but not powered for subgroup analysis. NCCN recommends adjuvant immunotherapy for resected mucosal melanoma. Level 2A evidence

survival_benefit.rationale: CheckMate-238: 4-year RFS 51.7% vs 41.2% for ipilimumab. Adjuvant nivolumab reduces recurrence risk ~30%. For T3N0M0 mucosal melanoma, high recurrence risk (>60% at 5 years) makes adjuvant therapy important. PD-L1 TPS 30% predicts better response

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for adjuvant melanoma. Nivolumab 480mg IV every 4 weeks for 1 year. Widely available at all oncology centers. Covered by insurance for melanoma indication

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated single-agent immunotherapy. Grade 3-4 AEs 14.4% in CheckMate-238. Immune-related AEs manageable with steroids. Favorable toxicity profile in 45-year-old with good baseline health

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 TPS 30% predicts enhanced response to anti-PD-1. BRAF WT status means no BRAF-targeted alternatives for adjuvant. Mucosal melanoma has lower TMB than cutaneous, which modestly reduces expected benefit vs cutaneous melanoma

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, releasing the immune checkpoint brake and enabling cytotoxic T cells to recognize and destroy residual melanoma cells post-surgery. PD-L1 TPS 30% indicates meaningful PD-L1 expression on tumor cells, facilitating PD-1/PD-L1 axis disruption.
biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression preferred but not required
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — adjuvant melanoma after complete resection

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Combination Checkpoint Inhibition)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate-067: 10-year landmark data published 2024 (NEJM). Median OS 71.9 months for combo. Mucosal melanoma subgroup ORR 37.1%. Post-hoc mucosal melanoma analyses from CheckMate-218 expanded access support efficacy. Strongest Phase 3 evidence in melanoma immunotherapy

survival_benefit.rationale: CheckMate-067 10-year OS: 43% for combo vs 37% nivo alone vs 19% ipi alone. For mucosal melanoma specifically: ORR 37.1% (vs 60% cutaneous). Median OS for mucosal melanoma with combo ~20-24 months. Significant improvement over historical mucosal melanoma survival of 9 months

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for unresectable/metastatic melanoma. Would be used if disease recurs or is unresectable. Off-label consideration for adjuvant use in very high-risk mucosal melanoma at experienced centers

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs 59% in CheckMate-067. Immune-mediated hepatitis, colitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies common. 36% treatment discontinuation due to toxicity. Significant toxicity burden but manageable in young 45-year-old patient

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 TPS 30% supports anti-PD-1 component. Combination overcomes some limitations of low TMB in mucosal melanoma. CTLA-4 blockade adds complementary immune activation independent of PD-L1 status. BRAF WT makes immunotherapy the primary systemic approach

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual checkpoint inhibition: nivolumab blocks PD-1 on T cells while ipilimumab blocks CTLA-4, together providing synergistic immune activation. Ipilimumab enhances T cell priming in lymph nodes while nivolumab releases the brakes in the tumor microenvironment. This complementary mechanism is particularly important in mucosal melanoma where low TMB limits single-agent anti-PD-1 efficacy.
biomarker_requirements: None required (PD-L1 TPS 30% is favorable but not mandatory)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — unresectable or metastatic melanoma

treatments.name: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (Anti-PD-L1 + Anti-VEGF)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 single-arm study in mucosal melanoma (n=43) with 3-year follow-up. ORR 45%, median PFS 8.2 months. Specifically designed and powered for mucosal melanoma. Published in Clinical Cancer Research 2022 with 2024 update. Strong Phase 2 evidence but no Phase 3 comparison

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 45% in mucosal melanoma (vs 23% for single-agent anti-PD-1). Median PFS 8.2 months. Median OS not reached at initial analysis. Upper-site (head/neck) patients had median PFS 15.2 months vs 5.3 months for lower sites. NRAS-mutant patients showed 100% ORR (5/5)

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved for other indications but NOT approved for melanoma. Off-label use requires insurance authorization. Available at major academic centers. Phase 3 trial needed for formal melanoma approval

safety_profile.rationale: Grade >=3 AEs 25.6% — lower than nivo+ipi. Most common: elevated LDH, hypercholesterolemia, transaminitis. 1 treatment-related death (pneumonitis). Bevacizumab adds hypertension and proteinuria risk. Overall manageable profile

biomarker_match.rationale: Exceptionally well-matched: patient is NRAS Q61K mutant (100% ORR in NRAS subgroup), PD-L1 TPS 30% (favorable for anti-PD-L1), and has upper-site mucosal melanoma (head/neck, median PFS 15.2 months). Triple biomarker alignment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Atezolizumab is an anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody that blocks PD-L1 on tumor cells, reversing immune evasion. Bevacizumab is an anti-VEGF antibody that normalizes tumor vasculature, improving T cell infiltration and reducing immunosuppressive VEGF signaling in the tumor microenvironment. The combination overcomes VEGF-mediated immune exclusion particularly relevant in mucosal melanoma.

key_evidence.study_name: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab in Mucosal Melanoma Phase II

biomarker_requirements: NRAS mutation strongly predictive (100% ORR in subgroup)
Upper mucosal site (head/neck) predictive of better PFS
treatments.availability: Phase 2 Trial Data — off-label at academic centers; both agents FDA-approved for other indications

treatments.name: Adjuvant Postoperative Radiation Therapy (IMRT/Proton Beam)

evidence_level.rationale: Meta-analysis of >1500 patients demonstrates 45% reduction in local recurrence with adjuvant RT. Multiple retrospective series and NCCN guidelines support adjuvant RT for head and neck mucosal melanoma. Level 2A evidence. No Phase 3 RCT feasible due to disease rarity

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant RT significantly improves local control (3-year local control up to 90%) but does NOT improve overall survival in most series. Primary benefit is preventing debilitating local recurrence in the oral cavity. OS remains driven by distant metastases

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at all radiation oncology centers. Proton beam therapy available at ~40 centers in the US. Carbon ion therapy very limited (Japan, Germany, few others). Standard IMRT is first-line RT approach

safety_profile.rationale: Oral cavity RT causes mucositis (90%), xerostomia (60-70%), dysphagia, taste changes, dental complications. IMRT reduces toxicity vs conventional RT. Proton beam further reduces dose to surrounding structures. Manageable but impacts quality of life

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable regardless of molecular profile. Radiation works via DNA damage independent of BRAF/NRAS/KIT status. Recommended for all resected head and neck mucosal melanoma irrespective of biomarkers

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon or particle beam radiation induces DNA double-strand breaks in residual tumor cells, causing cell death. IMRT allows precise dose conformality to the tumor bed while sparing adjacent critical structures (mandible, salivary glands). Proton beam therapy provides superior dose distribution with sharp Bragg peak falloff, reducing exit dose to normal tissue.

key_evidence.study_name: Meta-analysis of adjuvant RT in head and neck mucosal melanoma

treatments.availability: Standard of Care — IMRT widely available; proton beam at specialized centers

treatments.name: Temozolomide (Cytotoxic Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT comparing temozolomide vs dacarbazine in advanced melanoma (n=305). Established chemotherapy with decades of experience. Not melanoma-subtype specific. Considered last-line after immunotherapy and targeted therapy failure

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 7.7 months (TMZ) vs 6.4 months (DTIC). ORR 13.5% (CR 2.6%). Minimal survival benefit. In post-immunotherapy era, chemotherapy is strictly last-line. Real-world post-ICI data: median PFS 1.9 months, OS 6.0 months. Inferior to all immunotherapy and targeted approaches

accessibility.rationale: Oral administration (convenient). Widely available globally. Generic and inexpensive. No molecular testing required. Can be prescribed at any oncology center. Universal insurance coverage

safety_profile.rationale: Myelosuppression is dose-limiting: lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia. Nausea/vomiting manageable with antiemetics. Fatigue and constipation common. Moderate toxicity profile. Oral route is convenient

biomarker_match.rationale: Biomarker-independent alkylating agent. Works regardless of BRAF/NRAS/KIT status. Universal applicability as chemotherapy backbone. MGMT methylation may predict response but is not required for use

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent that methylates guanine residues in DNA (O6 and N7 positions), causing DNA mismatch and strand breaks that trigger tumor cell apoptosis. Unlike dacarbazine, temozolomide crosses the blood-brain barrier, providing CNS activity relevant for melanoma brain metastasis prophylaxis.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase III TMZ vs DTIC in Advanced Melanoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved — widely available generic oral chemotherapy

treatments.name: Binimetinib (MEK1/2 Inhibitor for NRAS Q61K)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 NEMO trial (n=402) demonstrated PFS superiority of binimetinib vs dacarbazine in NRAS-mutant melanoma. FDA approved as part of combination for BRAF-mutant melanoma but evidence base for NRAS monotherapy is Phase 3 level. Not specifically studied in mucosal melanoma subpopulation

survival_benefit.rationale: NEMO trial: median PFS 2.8 months (binimetinib) vs 1.5 months (DTIC). ORR 15.2% vs 6.8%. OS not significantly different. Modest PFS benefit. In pre-treated patients, PFS may extend to 5.5 months with durable responses lasting median 11 months

accessibility.rationale: Binimetinib (Mektovi) FDA-approved for BRAF V600E/K melanoma in combination with encorafenib. Off-label use for NRAS-mutant melanoma requires justification. Available at most oncology centers but insurance coverage for NRAS indication varies

safety_profile.rationale: NEMO trial: grade 3+ AEs 32%. CPK elevation (24%), rash (20%), diarrhea (18%), nausea (16%). Ocular toxicity (serous retinopathy) in 3%. Cardiac toxicity (LVEF decrease) in 4%. Manageable but requires monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has NRAS Q61K — a codon 61 hotspot mutation that drives constitutive MAPK pathway activation, the precise target of MEK inhibition. NRAS Q61K is among the most common NRAS mutations. Direct biomarker-to-target alignment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Binimetinib is a selective, allosteric inhibitor of MEK1 and MEK2 kinases, which are downstream effectors of the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) signaling cascade. In NRAS Q61K-mutant melanoma, the constitutively active NRAS drives uncontrolled MAPK signaling; MEK inhibition blocks this oncogenic driver at the pathway level.
biomarker_requirements: NRAS mutation (Q61R, Q61K, Q61L, or other activating mutations)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (for BRAF-mutant melanoma with encorafenib) — off-label for NRAS-mutant

treatments.name: Camrelizumab + Apatinib (Anti-PD-1 + Anti-VEGFR2)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 single-arm study (n=28 evaluable) in advanced mucosal melanoma. Published 2024. ORR 42.9%, DCR 82.1%, median PFS 8.05 months. Consistent with atezo+bev and axitinib+toripalimab data supporting anti-PD1+anti-VEGF in mucosal melanoma. No Phase 3 comparison

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 42.9% and DCR 82.1% are among the best response rates seen in mucosal melanoma. Median PFS 8.05 months exceeds single-agent checkpoint inhibitor PFS. Consistent with other anti-PD1+anti-VEGFR data showing ~45% ORR. RTK/RAS pathway mutations identified as predictive biomarker

accessibility.rationale: Camrelizumab and apatinib are approved in China but NOT FDA-approved. Not available in US/EU outside clinical trials. Access limited to participating sites in Asia. Not a realistic option for most Western patients

safety_profile.rationale: Acceptable toxicity profile with manageable AEs. Apatinib causes hypertension, hand-foot syndrome, proteinuria. Camrelizumab has unique AE of reactive cutaneous capillary endothelial proliferation (RCCEP). Overall toxicity similar to other anti-PD1+TKI combinations

biomarker_match.rationale: RTK/RAS pathway genes identified as predictive biomarkers — patient's NRAS Q61K mutation and KIT amplification are both in this pathway. TCR diversity and TMB also predictive. PD-L1 TPS 30% supports anti-PD-1. Excellent biomarker alignment for this combination

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Camrelizumab is a humanized anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody restoring T cell anti-tumor immunity. Apatinib is a selective VEGFR2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor that suppresses tumor angiogenesis and normalizes tumor vasculature, improving immune cell infiltration. The combination addresses both immune checkpoint and angiogenic pathways critical in mucosal melanoma.

key_evidence.study_name: Camrelizumab + Apatinib in Advanced Mucosal Melanoma Phase 2

biomarker_requirements: RTK/RAS pathway mutations predictive (NRAS Q61K qualifies)
TCR diversity and TMB as additional predictive biomarkers
notable_side_effects: reactive cutaneous capillary endothelial proliferation (RCCEP, camrelizumab-specific)
treatments.availability: Approved in China — not available in US/EU outside clinical trials

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Lenvatinib (Neoadjuvant/Perioperative)

evidence_level.rationale: NeoPlus Phase 2 trial (NCT04622566) specifically in resectable mucosal melanoma. Neoadjuvant lenvatinib 20mg + pembrolizumab 200mg q3w x 2 cycles, then surgery, then adjuvant pembrolizumab. Moderate anti-tumor efficacy demonstrated. Updated results presented at ASCO 2024

survival_benefit.rationale: NeoPlus: moderate anti-tumor efficacy in resectable mucosal melanoma. No mature survival data yet. Concept of neoadjuvant immunotherapy reducing recurrence risk is supported by SWOG S1801 data (cutaneous melanoma: EFS HR 0.58). Perioperative approach may improve on adjuvant-only strategy

accessibility.rationale: Phase 2 trial — requires enrollment at participating sites. Both agents FDA-approved individually for other indications. Off-label perioperative use at academic centers possible. Insurance authorization challenging for neoadjuvant combination

safety_profile.rationale: NeoPlus: hypertension 50%, proteinuria 46%, increased LDH 35%, hypothyroidism 31%. Lenvatinib adds significant anti-VEGF toxicity (bleeding risk concerning near surgical field in oral cavity). Neoadjuvant approach means toxicity occurs before surgery, potentially complicating wound healing

biomarker_match.rationale: Mucosal melanoma-specific trial. PD-L1 TPS 30% favorable for pembrolizumab. Lenvatinib targets VEGFR/FGFR relevant to mucosal melanoma biology. NRAS Q61K may predict anti-VEGF synergy (as seen in atezo+bev data). Good profile alignment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) restores T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha, RET, and KIT. Lenvatinib normalizes tumor vasculature, reduces immunosuppressive myeloid cells, and enhances T cell infiltration. Neoadjuvant administration exposes the intact tumor to the immune system, potentially generating stronger anti-tumor immunity before surgical removal.
biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 status assessed but not required for enrollment

treatments.name: Imatinib (KIT Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple Phase 2 trials specifically in KIT-altered mucosal/acral melanoma. Best evidence from Carvajal et al. (JCO 2013, n=25). FDA approved for GIST/CML but off-label for melanoma. Critical distinction: KIT mutations show 64% response in mucosal melanoma, but KIT amplification alone shows poor response

survival_benefit.rationale: For KIT AMPLIFIED (this patient's profile): median TTP 3.4 months, median OS 11.9 months. ORR only for KIT-mutated tumors (not amplification). Key limitation: KIT amplification without mutation predicts poor imatinib response. 77% DCR for mutated vs 18% for amplified-only in landmark trial

accessibility.rationale: Imatinib is widely available, generic formulations exist, relatively affordable. FDA approved for CML/GIST. Off-label melanoma use requires molecular justification. Most oncologists familiar with dosing and monitoring

safety_profile.rationale: Well-established safety profile from CML/GIST experience. Common AEs: edema (70%), fatigue (50%), nausea (50%), rash (30%). Grade 3-4 AEs uncommon (<10%). Generally well-tolerated long-term. One of the best-tolerated targeted therapies

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has KIT AMPLIFICATION, not KIT mutation. This is a critical distinction — imatinib is effective for KIT-mutated melanoma (exon 11 L576P, exon 13 K642E) but NOT for KIT amplification alone. Amplification without activating mutation does not predict response. Poor biomarker match for this patient

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Imatinib is a small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor that competitively binds the ATP-binding pocket of KIT (CD117), BCR-ABL, and PDGFR. In KIT-mutant melanoma, constitutive KIT activation drives proliferation through RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT pathways; imatinib blocks this oncogenic signaling. However, KIT amplification alone (without activating mutation) does not create the structural dependency that imatinib can exploit.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Imatinib for KIT-Altered Mucosal/Acral Melanoma (Carvajal et al.)

biomarker_requirements: KIT amplification alone predicts poor response
Patient has KIT amplification only — limited expected benefit
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for CML/GIST — off-label for KIT-altered melanoma

treatments.name: Carbon Ion Radiotherapy (Definitive or Adjuvant)

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective case series from Japanese and German institutions. Largest series: 72 cases of head and neck mucosal melanoma treated with carbon ion therapy, 5-year local control 84.1%. No randomized comparisons with photon RT. Level 3 evidence from single-institution retrospective data

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year local control rate 84.1% (superior to photon RT ~60-70%). Higher biological effectiveness (RBE 2-3x photons) overcomes melanoma's relative radioresistance. However, overall survival still limited by distant metastases. Local control improvement meaningful for oral cavity QoL

accessibility.rationale: Extremely limited: ~12 facilities worldwide (Japan: NIRS/QST, Gunma, Hyogo; Germany: HIT, MIT; few others). Not available in US. Requires international travel. Cost $30,000-60,000 per course. Not covered by US insurance. Significant logistical barriers

safety_profile.rationale: Superior dose conformality reduces normal tissue damage vs photon RT. Bragg peak allows precise dose delivery to tumor bed with sharp falloff. Less mucositis, xerostomia, and late toxicity than photon IMRT. Few serious AEs reported in published series

biomarker_match.rationale: Biomarker-independent radiation modality. High-LET carbon ions are effective regardless of molecular profile. The high RBE is particularly relevant for melanoma, which is considered relatively radioresistant to conventional photons. Universal applicability

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carbon ion beams deliver high-linear energy transfer (LET) radiation that causes dense, clustered DNA damage (complex double-strand breaks) that is difficult for tumor cells to repair, unlike the sparse damage from photon radiation. The Bragg peak allows precise dose deposition at the tumor site with minimal exit dose. The high relative biological effectiveness (RBE of 2-3) overcomes melanoma's intrinsic radioresistance.

key_evidence.study_name: Carbon Ion Therapy for Head and Neck Mucosal Melanoma (NIRS Retrospective)

treatments.availability: Research/Specialized — ~12 centers worldwide (Japan, Germany primarily); not available in US

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Cabozantinib (Anti-PD-1 + Multi-TKI)

evidence_level.rationale: Randomized Phase 2 trial (NCT05111574) actively recruiting at UCSF and MD Anderson. Compares adjuvant nivolumab alone vs nivolumab + cabozantinib in resected mucosal melanoma. No mature results yet. Based on strong preclinical rationale for VEGFR/MET/AXL inhibition synergy with anti-PD-1

survival_benefit.rationale: No mature efficacy data yet. Rationale based on cabozantinib's anti-VEGFR/MET/AXL activity enhancing immunotherapy. In advanced RCC, nivo+cabo showed significant OS improvement. Hypothesis is similar synergy in mucosal melanoma's VEGF-high microenvironment

accessibility.rationale: Active recruiting Phase 2 trial at select US academic centers. Requires trial enrollment. Both agents individually FDA-approved for other indications. Off-label combination possible but limited insurance coverage

safety_profile.rationale: From CheckMate-9ER (RCC): grade 3-4 AEs in 61%. Cabozantinib adds diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, hypertension. Nivolumab adds immune-related AEs. Combined toxicity significant but manageable in young patient

biomarker_match.rationale: Mucosal melanoma-specific trial. Cabozantinib targets VEGFR (relevant to mucosal melanoma angiogenesis), MET and AXL (resistance mechanisms to immunotherapy). PD-L1 TPS 30% supports anti-PD-1 component. Not KIT-specific but addresses broader resistance mechanisms

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR2, MET, and AXL — three pathways implicated in mucosal melanoma angiogenesis, metastasis, and immune evasion. Combined with nivolumab (anti-PD-1), it aims to normalize the tumor vasculature, overcome immune resistance, and enhance T cell trafficking into the tumor.

key_evidence.study_name: NCT05111574 — Adjuvant Nivolumab vs Nivolumab + Cabozantinib in Mucosal Melanoma

treatments.availability: Phase 2 Clinical Trial — actively recruiting at UCSF and MD Anderson

treatments.name: Lifileucel (TIL Cell Therapy — Amtagvi)

evidence_level.rationale: FDA approved February 2024 (accelerated) for unresectable/metastatic melanoma post-anti-PD-1. Phase 2 C-144-01 trial (n=73 evaluable): ORR 31.5%. 5-year follow-up shows durable responses (median DOR 36.5 months). First cellular therapy approved for solid tumors. Mucosal melanoma patients included in trial

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 31.5% in heavily pretreated melanoma (4.1% CR, 27.4% PR). Median DOR 36.5 months — exceptional durability. Responses deepened over time. For a salvage therapy after anti-PD-1 failure, this represents meaningful clinical benefit. Limited mucosal melanoma-specific data

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved but extremely limited availability. Requires certified treatment centers, tumor harvesting surgery, 3-4 week manufacturing, lymphodepleting chemotherapy, and ICU-level monitoring. Cost ~$515,000. Available at ~30 authorized treatment centers in US

safety_profile.rationale: Boxed Warning: treatment-related mortality, prolonged severe cytopenia, severe infection, cardiopulmonary and renal impairment. Grade 3+ AEs in 95.5%, Grade 4+ in 87.8%. Requires lymphodepleting conditioning (cyclophosphamide/fludarabine). Significant but acceptable in salvage setting

biomarker_match.rationale: Biomarker-agnostic approach — TIL therapy uses patient's own tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, bypassing need for specific mutations. Low TMB in mucosal melanoma may reduce TIL neoantigen targets, though TMB-independent mechanisms of TIL recognition exist. PD-L1 30% suggests some immune recognition

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lifileucel consists of autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) harvested from the patient's tumor, expanded ex vivo to billions of cells, and reinfused after lymphodepleting chemotherapy. These TILs recognize tumor-specific neoantigens and mediate direct cytotoxic killing of melanoma cells. The massive number of tumor-reactive T cells can overcome immune evasion mechanisms that limit checkpoint inhibitors.

key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Clinical Cancer Research

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (accelerated, February 2024) — limited to ~30 authorized centers in US

treatments.name: Nilotinib (Second-Generation KIT Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: TEAM Phase 2 trial: ORR 26.2%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 18 months in KIT-altered melanoma. Slightly better outcomes than imatinib in some cohorts. Mucosal melanoma patients comprised 20-71% of trial populations. Phase 2 evidence only

survival_benefit.rationale: TEAM trial: ORR 26.2%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 18 months. Modest improvement over imatinib for KIT-altered melanoma overall. Same limitation applies: KIT amplification (this patient) predicts substantially worse response than KIT mutation

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for CML. Generic available. Off-label use for KIT-altered melanoma. Familiar drug for most oncologists. Insurance coverage for off-label use requires molecular justification

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated. Common AEs: rash, nausea, fatigue, headache. QTc prolongation risk requires ECG monitoring. Pancreatitis risk. Better GI tolerance than imatinib for some patients. Low rate of serious AEs

biomarker_match.rationale: Same limitation as imatinib: patient has KIT amplification only, not KIT mutation. KIT amplification without activating mutation predicts limited benefit from KIT-targeted TKIs. Would be better suited for patients with KIT exon 11/13 mutations

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nilotinib is a second-generation BCR-ABL/KIT inhibitor with 20-50x greater potency than imatinib against KIT in vitro. It binds the inactive conformation of KIT tyrosine kinase, blocking downstream proliferative signaling. May overcome some imatinib-resistant KIT mutations, though clinical significance for amplification-only disease remains limited.

key_evidence.study_name: TEAM Phase II Trial (Nilotinib in KIT-altered melanoma)

biomarker_requirements: KIT amplification alone predicts limited benefit (this patient)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for CML — off-label for KIT-altered melanoma

treatments.name: Binimetinib + Imatinib (MEK + KIT Dual Inhibition)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial (ASCO 2025 abstract TPS9605) evaluating binimetinib + imatinib in KIT-mutant melanoma. Very early stage — trial design presented, no results yet. Preclinical rationale for dual MAPK pathway blockade. Limited to Phase 2 design announcement

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical efficacy data available yet. Preclinical rationale suggests dual pathway blockade may overcome resistance seen with single-agent KIT inhibition. Case reports of intermittent binimetinib + nivolumab showing complete metabolic response in NRAS Q61K melanoma support the MEK component

accessibility.rationale: Phase 2 clinical trial enrollment required. Both agents individually available but combination not standard. Would require off-label use outside trial with limited supporting evidence. Available at select academic sites

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicities of both agents. MEK inhibitor AEs (rash, diarrhea, CPK elevation, ocular toxicity) plus KIT inhibitor AEs (edema, nausea, myelosuppression). Overlapping GI toxicity may limit tolerability. No combination safety data published

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has both NRAS Q61K (binimetinib target) and KIT amplification (imatinib target). Dual pathway targeting is conceptually attractive for this molecular profile. However, KIT amplification alone may not respond to imatinib, reducing the combination rationale to primarily MEK inhibition

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual targeting of the MAPK signaling cascade: binimetinib inhibits MEK1/2 downstream of NRAS Q61K, while imatinib blocks KIT-mediated signaling from the amplified KIT receptor. The combination aims to provide vertical pathway suppression at two nodes, potentially overcoming compensatory signaling that limits single-agent efficacy.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Binimetinib + Imatinib in KIT-Mutant Melanoma (ASCO 2025 TPS9605)
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology (ASCO 2025 Trial in Progress)

biomarker_requirements: NRAS mutation and KIT amplification (patient's profile — partial match)
treatments.availability: Phase 2 Clinical Trial (ASCO 2025 TPS9605) — enrollment stage

clinical_trials.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab Alone or With Cabozantinib to Prevent Mucosal Melanoma Return After Surgery
clinical_trials.early_results: No mature results. Rationale based on CheckMate-9ER (RCC) showing nivo+cabo superiority. Addresses critical unmet need in adjuvant mucosal melanoma.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab and Lenvatinib in Resectable Mucosal Melanoma (NeoPlus)
clinical_trials.early_results: ASCO 2024 update: moderate anti-tumor efficacy in resectable mucosal melanoma. Hypertension 50%, proteinuria 46%. Neoadjuvant approach aims for pathologic response before surgery.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Binimetinib Plus Imatinib in Patients with Unresectable KIT-Mutant Melanoma
biomarker_requirements: KIT mutation (note: patient has KIT amplification, eligibility may vary)
clinical_trials.early_results: Trial design presented at ASCO 2025. No efficacy data yet. Dual MAPK pathway blockade concept.

clinical_trials.title: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab in Unresectable/Metastatic Mucosal Melanoma (Confirmatory Trial)
biomarker_requirements: NRAS mutation may be predictive biomarker
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 2 demonstrated ORR 45%, median PFS 8.2 months. 3-year follow-up confirms durable efficacy. NRAS-mutant subgroup showed 100% ORR. Phase 3 confirmatory trial expected.

clinical_trials.title: Lifileucel TIL Therapy in Mucosal Melanoma Post-Anti-PD-1
clinical_trials.early_results: Overall melanoma ORR 31.5%, median DOR 36.5 months. Mucosal melanoma subgroup data being collected. FDA accelerated approval February 2024.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant PD-1 and LAG-3-targeting Bispecific Antibody and Other ICI Combinations in Resectable Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Published Nature Medicine 2025. Testing multiple neoadjuvant immunotherapy combinations including anti-PD-1/LAG-3 bispecific antibodies. Novel checkpoint combinations may address mucosal melanoma's immunotherapy resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade provides synergistic immune activation: ipilimumab enhances T cell priming while nivolumab releases effector T cell brakes. In mucosal melanoma, ORR 37.1% vs 23% for nivo alone. 10-year OS 43% for combo in overall melanoma. Critical for overcoming low-TMB immune evasion in mucosal melanoma.

VEGF inhibition normalizes tumor vasculature, enhances T cell infiltration, and reduces immunosuppressive myeloid cells. ORR 45% in mucosal melanoma. NRAS-mutant patients show 100% ORR. Head/neck mucosal melanoma has superior PFS (15.2 months). Addresses the unique VEGF-dependent biology of mucosal melanoma.

Surgery provides definitive local control; adjuvant IMRT reduces local recurrence by 45%; adjuvant nivolumab reduces systemic recurrence risk by ~30%. Triple-modality approach addresses both local and distant failure patterns in T3N0M0 mucosal melanoma. Sequencing: surgery first, concurrent or sequential RT + immunotherapy.

MEK inhibition may enhance anti-tumor immunity by increasing MHC class I expression and tumor antigen presentation. Case reports document complete metabolic response in NRAS Q61K melanoma with intermittent binimetinib + nivolumab. MEK inhibition may paradoxically enhance immunotherapy by promoting immunogenic cell death. Relevant for this NRAS Q61K patient.

Lenvatinib targets VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFR-alpha, RET, and KIT — addressing multiple angiogenic and signaling pathways in mucosal melanoma. Neoadjuvant approach may generate stronger anti-tumor immunity with intact primary tumor. KIT amplification in this patient may derive additional benefit from lenvatinib's KIT inhibitory activity.

supportive_care.approach: Structured Exercise and Physical Activity Program
supportive_care.evidence: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses demonstrate exercise reduces cancer-related fatigue (primary evidence from melanoma-specific feasibility study, Melanoma Research 2023). ACS guidelines recommend 150-300 min/week moderate activity for cancer survivors.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces cancer-related fatigue (first-line treatment per guidelines), improves quality of life during immunotherapy, maintains functional status, may enhance immunotherapy response through improved immune function and reduced chronic inflammation

supportive_care.evidence: PMC systematic review (2021) on nutritional interventions for melanoma. Research shows high-fiber diet (>20g/day) and diverse gut microbiome correlate with improved immunotherapy response. Patients with healthy gut microbiome diversity had 5x better response to anti-PD-1 therapy.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains body weight during treatment (weight stability predicts better OS), supports immune function, high-fiber diet promotes beneficial gut microbiome diversity associated with better immunotherapy outcomes. Avoid unnecessary antibiotics and probiotics during immunotherapy

supportive_care.evidence: Standard of care for oral cavity surgery patients per NCCN Head and Neck guidelines. Pre-surgical baseline assessment and post-surgical rehabilitation improve functional outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Preserves speech intelligibility after partial glossectomy, prevents aspiration pneumonia through swallowing exercises, maintains oral intake and nutritional status. Critical for quality of life after oral tongue surgery

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO/NCCN guidelines recommend distress screening and psychosocial support for all cancer patients. Mucosal melanoma's poor prognosis and visible location creates significant psychological burden.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses anxiety, depression, and cancer-related distress. 45-year-old facing rare aggressive cancer with disfiguring surgery and uncertain prognosis benefits from integrated psychosocial care. Cognitive behavioral therapy and mindfulness reduce distress

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Head and Neck guidelines mandate comprehensive dental evaluation before radiation therapy. Fluoride trays, dental extractions of compromised teeth, and sialogogues reduce RT-related complications.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents osteoradionecrosis of mandible (5-10% risk with oral cavity RT), manages xerostomia, preserves dental health during and after radiation. Must be initiated BEFORE starting radiation therapy
